questions.question:
A $4.00\text{ kg}$ rock is dropped from the top of a $22.5\text{ m}$ high building.

Calculate its momentum at the time it strikes the ground. ($g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2$)

A $2.50\text{ kg}$ rock is thrown upward from ground level at $12.0\text{ m/s}$. It travels to a maximum height, then returns to the ground.

Calculate its momentum as it strikes the ground. (Ignore air resistance.)
questions.explanation: Ignoring air resistance, the rock returns to ground level at the same speed it was thrown: $12.0\text{ m/s}$. $p = mv = 2.50 \times 12.0 = 30.0\text{ kg m/s}$.

questions.question:
A $3.20\text{ kg}$ rock is thrown upward from ground level, reaches a maximum height of $12.5\text{ m}$, then returns to the ground.

Calculate its momentum as it strikes the ground. ($g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2$)

A $1,500\text{ kg}$ test car is driving on a straight track. The car's driver steps on the gas pedal, causing a constant forward force to act on the vehicle. This force causes the car to speed up from $15.0\text{ m/s}$ to $25.0\text{ m/s}$ over a short distance.

What is the magnitude of the impulse experienced by the car during this increase in speed?
questions.explanation: Impulse $= \Delta p = m(v_f - v_i) = 1,500 \times (25.0 - 15.0) = 1,500 \times 10.0 = 1.50 \times 10^4\text{ N s}$.

questions.question:
A $1,400\text{ kg}$ car accelerates along a straight road, increasing its speed from $40.0\text{ km/h}$ to $85.0\text{ km/h}$.

What is the magnitude of the impulse experienced by the car?
questions.explanation: $v_i = 40.0 / 3.6 \approx 11.11\text{ m/s}$. $v_f = 85.0 / 3.6 \approx 23.61\text{ m/s}$. $\Delta v = 12.50\text{ m/s}$. Impulse $= m\Delta v = 1,400 \times 12.50 = 1.75 \times 10^4\text{ N s}$.

questions.question:
A $1,100\text{ kg}$ car accelerates from rest to a speed of $12.0\text{ m/s}$ over a time interval of $3.20\text{ s}$.

Calculate the magnitude of the force that the car's tires exerted on the road.
questions.explanation: $\Delta p = 1,100 \times 12.0 = 13,200\text{ kg m/s}$. $F = \Delta p / t = 13,200 / 3.20 = 4,125\text{ N} \approx 4.13 \times 10^3\text{ N}$.

questions.question:
A $1,300\text{ kg}$ car traveling at $95.0\text{ km/h}$ slams on its brakes and comes to a complete stop in $3.80\text{ s}$.

Calculate the magnitude of the friction force experienced by the car during this braking period.

A $0.45\text{ kg}$ soccer ball is moving toward a player at $7.00\text{ m/s}$. The player kicks the ball, causing it to rebound in the opposite direction at $12.00\text{ m/s}$. The foot remains in contact with the ball for $1.50 \times 10^{-2}\text{ s}$.

What is the magnitude of the average force that the player's foot applies to the ball?
questions.explanation: Taking the rebound direction as positive: $\Delta v = 12.00 - (-7.00) = 19.00\text{ m/s}$. $\Delta p = 0.45 \times 19.00 = 8.55\text{ kg m/s}$. $F = 8.55 / 0.0150 = 570\text{ N} = 5.70 \times 10^2\text{ N}$.

questions.question:
A $2.50\text{ kg}$ package accidentally falls from a drone hovering at a height of $15.0\text{ m}$ and lands on the grass below.

Calculate the magnitude of the impulse experienced by the package due to gravity during its entire descent.
questions.explanation: $v_f^2 = 2gh = 2 \times 9.80 \times 15.0 = 294$. $v_f = \sqrt{294} \approx 17.15\text{ m/s}$. Impulse $= m\Delta v = 2.50 \times 17.15 \approx 42.9\text{ N s}$.

questions.question:
A $3.20\text{ kg}$ object slips off a ledge at a height of $12.0\text{ m}$ and falls to the surface below. Upon impact, the object comes to a complete stop in $0.250\text{ s}$.

What is the magnitude of the average net force that the ground exerts on the object to bring it to rest?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered multi-step impulse and momentum applications!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review problems involving direction changes and unit conversions (km/h to m/s).
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on using $F = \Delta p / t$ and combining kinematics with momentum.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: impulse-momentum theorem, kinematic equations for falling objects, and unit conversions.
